_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Symptomatic patients (New York Heart Association functional class greater than II)
Asymptomatic patients with pulmonary hypertension with moderate or severe stenosis
Absence of moderate to severe mitral regurgitation

preauth.label: Clinical notes with planned line of treatment

claim.label: Post procedure stills of ECHO with report

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure
Was the Echo report suggestive of mitral valve stenosis?

cpd_claim.q: Does the Post procedure still of ECHO show improvement of Mitral valve area?

common_queries: Echo report not showing mitral valve stenosis.
Clinical notes missing or not detailing history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure.
Post-procedure ECHO stills missing or not showing improvement in Mitral valve area.
Invoice/barcode of balloon used missing at claim stage.
NYHA functional class not documented or documented as II or less for symptomatic patients.
Left atrial thrombus or moderate to severe mitral regurgitation documented without justification for PMBV.

additional_information.special_empanelment: Cardiac Catheterization lab: Yes; CCU/ICCU: Yes; Qualified cardiologist (DM/DNB cardiology): Yes; Qualified cardiothoracic surgeon (MCh/DNB cardiovascular thoracic surgery): No
clinical_key_pointers: Treatment of mitral stenosis ranges from conservative medical management to closed mitral valvotomy and the more recent balloon mitral valvoplasty.
Closed mitral valvotomy is a well-established method for treatment of rheumatic mitral stenosis.

A number of large series have reported successful long-term relief of symptoms after operation. Substantial long-term improvement in the mitral valve area following closed valvotomy has been demonstrated.
Percutaneous mitral balloon valvuloplasty (PMBV) is an invasive procedure used to manage mitral stenosis. PMBV improves symptoms by increasing the mitral valve area and reduce mitral valve gradient.
Balloon mitral valvotomy is now the treatment of choice for many patients with symptomatic mitral stenosis.
Common Signs and Symptoms of Mitral Stenosis: Orthopnea, Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, Palpitations, Chest pain, Hemoptysis.

Common Signs of Mitral Stenosis: Loud first heart sound, Opening snap (OS) may be heard after the A2 component of the second heart sound, Mid-diastolic low pitch rumbling murmur with presystolic accentuation is heard after the opening snap. It is best heard with the bell of the stethoscope at the apex., Atrial fibrillation, Left parasternal heave, Tapping apical beat.
